per_clue.clue_text: Father in Berlin is entertaining American artist
per_clue.blog:
DAD (Father) A (American) IST (German for ‘is’ i.e. in Berlin)
.
Heavily influenced by WWI, DADAISTs rejected violence, war, nationalism and modern capitalist society, using nonsense and protest in their works. The whole point behind Dadaism was to prove that anything could be art if the artist declared it to be, which in turn proved that if everything could be art, then nothing could be art. This outraged many artists as Dadaism deemed their labour-intensive artworks meaningless.

In case you have all forgotten, Zebedee was the magic Jack-in-the-box; Dylan the rabbit; and Brian the snail. “Hello, dearhearts” was apparently Ermintrude’s catchphrase.

per_clue.clue_text: Tax collector no longer charges double for drink
per_clue.blog: IR (tax collector no longer) in SPIT (double) This was one of my last two in. It is safe to say I worked on this clue for the better part of 20 minutes. I was pretty sure I understood the wordplay, but I was only sort of sure about IR (we have IRS here, and this has come up before), but I couldn’t think of a synonym of ‘double’ for the life of me. I tried DUET, leading to DUIRET, but that didn’t look like anything. Finally I saw SPIRIT, and worked my way back to SPIT. Okay, well I know ‘spitting image’, so I guess this is a related usage.

I think of PARCH as ‘to dry out’, whereas ‘dry’ means ‘to un-wet something’. Am I crazy? Perhaps.

per_clue.clue_text: Description of fair racecourse?
per_clue.blog: I had no idea what was going on here although AINTREE was obvious from ‘racecourse’. Google advises: “Aintree Fair” usually refers to the popular Aintree Antique and Collectors Fair, a large monthly event held at the iconic Aintree Racecourse near Liverpool. Who knew?  I had this down as two definitions and  thought it one of the most feeble of that type I have ever seen in a Times crossword because  they are linked to the same venue. On reflection I think it’s just a cryptic definition, but I still don’t care for it . Edit: Oh well, you can’t win ’em all! Thanks to vinyl1 for pointing out the correct wordplay: ‘fair’ = A IN TREE (fir).

per_clue.clue_text: Make money from openings provided by India's small enterprises
per_clue.blog: The idea here is that you look at the last three words of the clue and notice that one is capitalised and the second and third aren’t, so you would describe the openings as “capital I [followed by] s and e. Whether CAPITALISE is actually a synonym for making money is moot, as it means providing capital or converting into capital, which doesn’t necessarily equate to making money (investments can go up and down). Close enough though. This clue was my nemesis, causing me to stare bleakly at the unhelpful checking letters at the end as the minutes ticked round, trying to get an IF in there; an I in there, and not understanding why the word couldn’t be something to do with financing, or monetising, or profiting.

This is with reference to a story of men caught raking a pond for kegs of smuggled brandy, who feigned madness to fool the revenue men, by saying they were raking out the moon. I don’t think I have come across any of this before so although I was able to guess the answer from checkers the clue had me baffled until I researched it.

Today the wordplay was too tricky for me so I resorted to aids. ENG for English and REP for Republican are quite rare in wordplay by comparison with the ubiquitous E and R, and just didn’t occur to me.

per_clue.clue_text: Church area unkempt for a long time

per_clue.clue_text: Wanderer compos mentis in Glesga?

per_clue.clue_text: Rock singer’s learning, ready for Romania
per_clue.blog: Our favourite singer on top of a rock (by the Rhine) though as she appears regularly in grids you are probably all quite familiar with her by now. What I didn’t know is that the legend arose because there was a murmuring sound created by the currents and a waterfall. Another nice clue, we add the plural of the currency of Romania (LEI) to LORE for learning. For newbies, you will soon disregard the more usual meaning of “ready” for its slang expression meaning “money” whenever you see the word.

per_clue.clue_text: Cheap adhesive

per_clue.clue_text: Alsatian? That’s poodle transformed with CGI!

per_clue.clue_text: Uranium and fruit devoured by an android

per_clue.blog: Hidden.

per_clue.clue_text: Gaseous element consumed in recent delivery
per_clue.blog: What is needed here is a recent delivery of the baby variety. NEON gives us our gaseous element to which we add ATE for “consumed”.

per_clue.clue_text: More mature one training at school between runs
per_clue.blog: Another very gentle one. Run can be R so “runs” can be RR. Into that we insert I and PE for “training at school”.

per_clue.clue_text: Delays speaker aboard westbound train
per_clue.blog: Clever wordplay here, though again, the literal allows for a fairly easy biff once you have a few checkers. Speaker = ORATOR, which goes inside (“aboard”) a reversal (“westbound”) of AIM, in that sense of “train”.

per_clue.clue_text: Make careless error, with which mood becomes low?

per_clue.clue_text: Go-go dancing with elderly politician in the past
per_clue.blog: A great anagram (“dancing”) of GOGO and ELDERLY. From all accounts, if go-go dancing had been invented at the turn of 20th Century then LLOYD GEORGE might well have been up for such entertainment.

per_clue.clue_text: Bible incomplete? Voice criticism

per_clue.clue_text: Smoke starts off in garage filling vehicle

per_clue.clue_text: Queen once overwhelmed by strain hides here
per_clue.blog: Crosswordland is replete with kings and queens of all shapes and sizes. ANNE is our chosen monarch du jour which is inside (overwhelmed) by TRY for “strain”.

per_clue.clue_text: In which one may grow the best cannabis?
per_clue.blog: FLOWER can have a meaning of “the best of anything” as in “Flower of Scotland”. I was looking for an equivalent expression meaning “the worst of anything” to apply to England Rugby’s efforts at the weekend, but moving swiftly on to the matter in hand, we add POT for cannabis and arrive at a receptacle in which we might grow something.

per_clue.clue_text: Concerning number of roach trails seen at intervals?
per_clue.blog: “Interval” clue.

per_clue.clue_text: Worker in ruse endlessly referencing Buddhist works
per_clue.blog: Ruse = TRICK from which we subtract the last letter and insert our favourite type of worker (ANT). I started researching TANTRA but very soon realised it is an extremely dense and complicated subject. Its association with sex by the western world appears to be something of a sideshow.

per_clue.clue_text: Success keeps me bold, playing in tournament ...
per_clue.blog: A “success” is a WIN into which is inserted an anagram (playing) of ME + BOLD. Nice clue…

per_clue.clue_text: ... serving for the match?
per_clue.blog: …as is this one.  A bit like “ready” for cash, whenever one sees “match” one should always be on the alert for something to do with nuptials. A fairly gentle cryptic clue but none the worse for that.

per_clue.clue_text: Figure toned and rather developed
per_clue.blog: Another nice surface with easy wordplay. “Developed” is our anagram indicator, which we apply to TONED and RATHER.

per_clue.clue_text: Beaten about at noon, had little sleep
per_clue.blog: CAPPED can be a synonym for “beaten” into which is placed AT (in plain sight) and N for “noon”. “Little” here in the sense of a short specific period of sleep.

per_clue.clue_text: Listing omitting a great historic city
per_clue.blog: Listing is LEANING from which A is omitted. RAD is a fairly modern synonym for “great”.

per_clue.clue_text: Violent wind and low cloud right by a lake
per_clue.blog: The setter is giving us a bit more help by providing a description of the type of wind we are looking for, and MISTRAL is one of the better known, being a strong, cold, dry northwesterly wind from the Massif Central/Alps down the Rhone valley into the Mediterranean. The wordplay is MIST for “low cloud” + R + A + L.

per_clue.clue_text: Laugh from retired officer engaged in tiresome task

per_clue.clue_text: Function in business without Latin

It’s something or other to do with hypoteneuses and adjacent sides. When I was 17 I knew all about them, but have never ever once had cause to refer to them since, until now anyhow, which says something about education. If only they had taught me how to get a doctor’s appointment instead… although to be fair that wasn’t difficult, in those days.

per_clue.clue_text: Colour endless in a divine English novel

Instead we have our second favourite 4/4 crossword novel.  “Devine” as a noun meaning a person skilled in divine things/theologian gives us DD (Doctor of Divinity) and the colour we need is AMBER which loses its final letter (endless). An “a” in plain sight and “E” for English completes the recipe.

per_clue.clue_text: Blakey for one is to strive for creative skill
per_clue.blog: I didn’t know who ART Blakey was but with A-T as my checking letters was able to assume that there was such a person. An important jazz musician, he is credited with keeping the jazz scene going during the changing tastes of the 1970s. To ART we add IS (in plain sight) and TRY (strive) for our definition.

per_clue.clue_text: Nothing stops Kelvin, a debauchee, in pub activity
per_clue.blog: Not sure KARAOKE is limited to pubs in Japan but it makes a nice surface. William Thomson (Lord Kelvin) determined the correct value of absolute zero as being -273.15 Celsius or -459.67 Farenheit leading to the kelvin (K) being the base unit for temperature. After K, A RAKE (a debauchee) is “stopped by” (i.e. has within it) O for “nothing”.

per_clue.blog: A (adult) plus BASHED (struck).

per_clue.clue_text: Jousting weapon returned to lake for knight
per_clue.blog: Easy to biff once you see “jousting weapon” and “knight”. “Returned to lake” is the slightly tricky bit of the w/p giving us L OT, which is added to LANCE.

per_clue.clue_text: Spaniards here competed in scoreless draw
per_clue.blog: Another nice surface. A city in northern Spain, I first came across it from the Woody Allen film Vicky Cristina Barcelona. Hopefully OO as a “scoreless draw” in football/soccer will not cause problems for our transatlantic friends. Inside we place VIED (competed).

per_clue.clue_text: Show without Tarzan struggling to entertain Virginia and Georgia
per_clue.blog: “Without” can be EX as in “ex dividend”. Maybe not very common but it is there in the dictionary. *(TARZAN) then struggles to enclose VA and GA, abbreviations for two US states. Another surface which is a bit nonsensical but caused a smile here.

per_clue.clue_text: Son leaves belt in corner
per_clue.blog: This is STRAP for “belt” without S for “son”.

per_clue.clue_text: Excited seeing leg — not small — in flimsy robe
per_clue.blog: Maybe I was just in a good mood when doing this, but this one even managed to elicit a chuckle. “Excited” is our anagram indicator and – without the S for “small” – *(SEEING LEG) is the anagram fodder.

per_clue.clue_text: Dance party taking place next to European capital
per_clue.blog: There are an awful lot of parties in Crosswordland to choose from, but we can normally rely on “European” being E. Not here, where RIGA is our European capital, which is next to DO ON for “party taking place”. A lively jig-like dance for one couple. Known to me from these things. Possibly a tricky clue if you have never heard of it.

per_clue.clue_text: Help mounted soldiers cutting spring migration
per_clue.blog: AID is reversed (mounted, in a down clue) then OR (ordinary ranks) comes within (cutting) SPA for “spring”.

per_clue.clue_text: Folk performer, crafty poet leading reindeer
per_clue.blog: I got the DANCER reference straightaway (I can never remember all the other reindeeer but didn’t need to). MORRIS was a bit later coming, biffed in fact, but then I saw that “craft-y” presumably refers to the Arts and Crafts movement of which William Morris was a towering figure. I must admit to not knowing he was a poet in addition to everything else he did, but apparently he was offered the Poet Laureateship in 1871.

per_clue.clue_text: Nutty green apple finally prepared for cake
per_clue.blog: Can green apples ever be nutty in taste? I’m prepared to believe it for this amusing surface, and offbeat literal, though it is rather a derogatory expression. I guess every walk of life has its reasonable folks and those at the margins. Tricky wordplay as a preposition (FOR) is part of the fodder for the anagram (prepared) along with CAKE and the last letter of “apple”.  My last one in.

per_clue.clue_text: Just for Latinists, this with others has to go round
per_clue.blog: I saw the HIC for “for Latinists, this” but wanted “etc” for “with others” rather than the correct ET AL

per_clue.clue_text: Complaint NHS department’s well able to explain?
per_clue.blog: FLU + ENT. Simple but neat.

per_clue.clue_text: Duke in good health carrying spades
per_clue.blog: Newbies will probably wonder what “duke” has to do with “fist”. Rare in normal life, it does appear from time to time in puzzles: they are indeed synonyms. If we are in good health we are FIT which goes around S (spades as in a suit in cards).

per_clue.clue_text: Plot involving alien laser beam accidentally revealed?
per_clue.blog: Into the garden here where “plot” gives us BED, into which we put our favourite alien, ET, and RAY for “laser beam”. Obviously one can intentionally betray someone, but there is a separate sense of accidentally revealing something as well.

per_clue.clue_text: Fools in America delinquent once hit
per_clue.blog: Every time I see TED for a delinquent or here “delinquent once” I think of Dorset Jimbo, a sadly late but frequent contributor to TfTT who would always object to such a connection. Here the term goes on the end of a long charade starting with two ASSes; IN (in plain sight) and A as one of the accepted abbreviations for America.

per_clue.clue_text: Almost do away with range of knowledge on New York City
per_clue.blog: Experienced hands will immediately lift and separate between New York and City, and indeed we are transported to Ireland for the literal, which is made up of all but the last letter of KILL (do away with); KEN (range of knowledge) and NY.

per_clue.clue_text: Bishop in argument about new Victorian poet
per_clue.blog: B plus ROWING (in argument) about N.

per_clue.clue_text: Place abnormally loaded with hidden gold
per_clue.blog: I guess this could be a semi &lit as well. Either way it is another good clue with smoothness and solvability. Inside (hidden) an anagram (abnormally) of *(LOADED) there is OR, the tincture gold or yellow in heraldry.

per_clue.clue_text: Shock where old hand guzzles gallon
per_clue.blog: STAGER around G for “gallon”.

checks.detail: indicator 'guzzles' for op 'container': no DB row with type in ['container', 'insertion']

per_clue.clue_text: Irrigation of Algarve at regular intervals over time
per_clue.blog: Not a word I knew but the LAV bit is gettable (every other letter (at regular intervals) of ALGARVE) and then I guess you have to decide whether “time” is eon, era or the correct AGE.

per_clue.clue_text: American not eating in land around Lomé
per_clue.blog: Slightly panicked when I saw this as I couldn’t quite remember where Lome is. It’s a good example of how important it is to “slice” the clue at the right point between wordplay and definition. Here “in” is a crucial part of the literal, as what Brits would call “takeaway” is “to go” to those Stateside. On a visit to California once I did indeed go into a restaurant and ask if they did “takeaway”.  To this day I can remember the oddness of struggling to explain what I was asking for.
